content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Public Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660            322-5901          322-6441             322-6444

October 31, 1983


Peter F. Schabarum, Supervisor
Los Angeles County
856 Hall of Administration
Los Angeles, CA 90012

                                    Re: Your Letter of August 2, 1983
                                        Advice Letter No. A-83-244

Dear Supervisor Schabarum:

     Roger Brown has referred your letter of August 2, 1983, to
the Commission's Legal Division so that we may advise you as to
the correct method of valuing and reporting the gifts of tickets
which you received from USC and UCLA. The Political Reform Act
(the"Act")1/ requires that public officials such as
yourself2/ report the receipt of gifts3/ from any given
donor when the value of the gift or gifts is $50 or more during
the period covered by the official's Statement of Economic
Interests.4/

     The term "gift," as used in the Act, is defined in Section
82028 as follows:

     "Gift" means, except as provided in subdivision
(b), any payment to the extent that consideration of
equal or greater value is not received and includes a
rebate or discount in the price of anything of value
unless the rebate or discount is made in the regular
course of business to members of the public without
regard to official status. Any person, other than a
defendant in a criminal action, who claims that a

_____________________

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless specified
otherwise.

2/ Section 87200.

3/ Section 87207(a)(1).

4/ Section 87203 requires that officials file annual
statements covering the previous calendar year.

Peter F. Schabarum, Supervisor
October 31, 1983
Page 2


     payment is not a gift by reason of receipt of
consideration has the burden of proving that the
consideration received is of equal or greater value.

     (b)  The term "gift" does not include:

                         * * *

     (2)  Gifts which are not used and which,
within 30 days after receipt, are returned to the
donor or delivered to a charitable organization
without being claimed as a charitable contribution
for tax purposes;

                         * * *

     For example when a gift of season tickets to USC football
games is received, which may have a value of $400, you may
return them unused within 30 days (but prior to the first game)
if you do not wish to receive a gift.  You could also give them
to a charity within the same time limits and not receive a gift,
so long as you claim no charitable deduction.

     If you wish to retain the gift, but desire to avoid
potential disqualification, you must reduce the gift's value to
below $250.  This may be accomplished by returning part of the
tickets, unused, within 30 days of receipt and prior to the
events for which the returned tickets are issued.  Or you may
pay5/ for all or a portion of the tickets so as to reduce the
net value of the gift below $250.  In the example here, you
would need to send USC a check for $151.  However, this must be
done within the 30 days of receipt of the tickets.  You cannot,
at a later date, pay for a portion of the tickets.

     In addition, when valuing the gift, you must take into
account the value of any parking passes or privileges and any
souvenir programs, etc., which you are given by the same donor.
Giving your tickets to friends or constituents does not alter
their value to you for reporting and disqualification purposes.

     On the other hand, if your spouse or other members of your
family personally receive USC tickets as well, you need not

_____________________
5/  Payment must be made from your personal funds.

Peter F. Schabarum, Supervisor
October 31, 1983
Page 3


include the value of those tickets as a gift to you unless they
do not use the tickets and turn them over to you.

     With the above information in mind, you may wish to review
your Statement of Economic Interests to determine whether these
gifts have been accurately reported.  Should you desire our
specific advice on your reporting requirements, we would be
pleased to render that advice to you.

     You should remember that any gift you receive which has a
value of $250 or more could form the basis for disqualification
for a period of 12 months from its receipt.5/  Consequently,
if the correct valuation of your gifts from USC and UCLA is $250
or more, then you may have to disqualify yourself during the
appropriate 12-month period.

     Should you have any questions regarding this letter, I may
be reached at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,



                                        Robert E. Leigh
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

REL:plh
cc:  John H. Larson, County Counsel

_________________________

     5/  Section 82030(a) defines "income" as including any
"gift."  Section 87103(c) states that a source of income (gift)
of $250 or more during the preceding 12 months will be the basis
for a disqualification if the other requirements of Section
87103 are met.

sections.question_synthetic: How should Supervisor Schabarum value and report gifts of tickets received from USC and UCLA under the Political Reform Act, and what are the thresholds and options for managing these gifts?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Gifts of $50 or more must be reported. To avoid disqualification, the gift value must be kept below $250 by returning unused tickets within 30 days, donating to charity, or paying for a portion. Gifts of $250 or more trigger mandatory disqualification for 12 months.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Standard FPPC advice letter with clear question context (implied from referenced August 2 letter) and detailed conclusion with examples. No explicit Q/A headers but content structure is clear. Footnotes provide statutory citations.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: How should Supervisor Schabarum value and report gifts of tickets received from USC and UCLA under the Political Reform Act, and what are the thresholds and options for managing these gifts?

CONCLUSION: Gifts of $50 or more must be reported. To avoid disqualification, the gift value must be kept below $250 by returning unused tickets within 30 days, donating to charity, or paying for a portion. Gifts of $250 or more trigger mandatory disqualification for 12 months.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises Supervisor Schabarum on reporting and valuing gifts of tickets from USC and UCLA. The letter explains gift definition requirements, reporting thresholds ($50), disqualification triggers ($250), and options for managing gift value through returns, charitable donations, or partial payment within 30 days of receipt.
